subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you will learn how to measure length – how long, how tall, how wide or how far something is. Measuring is one of the most useful skills in all of mathematics, and you will use it for the rest of your life. When Amina helps her mother cut cloth at the Hargeisa market, when Yusuf measures how far it is from his house to the masjid, or when Halima checks that a shelf is long enough for her books, they are all measuring length.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and whenever you can, pick up a real ruler and measure the very things we talk about. Mathematics about measuring is best learned with a ruler in your hand, not just by reading. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers, and your teacher will meet with you about twice a week to help with anything tricky.

Why does measuring matter so much? Length is everywhere in daily life. Traders measure cloth and rope to sell the right amount. Carpenters measure wood before they cut, because a piece cut too short is wasted. Farmers measure how far apart to plant seeds. Families measure children to see how much they have grown. Even builders raising the walls of a new home at Nuur Academy must measure carefully, or the building will not be straight. When you become confident with measuring length, you are gaining a skill that people all around you use every single day.

As you work, keep a ruler, a pencil and some paper beside you. Draw the lines, measure real objects, and say the measurements out loud. If you measure something wrongly, that is not a failure – it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Measuring well is a skill of the hands and the eyes as much as the mind, and like any skill, it grows stronger every time you use it.
Use the standard units centimetres (cm) and metres (m) to measure length.
Explain that there are 100 centimetres in 1 metre.
Use a ruler and a metre stick correctly, starting from the right place.
Estimate a length first, then measure to check how close you were.
Tell the difference between length, width and height.
Compare and order objects using longer, shorter, taller, wider and narrower.
Draw lines of an exact length using a ruler.

concepts.title: What Is Length?
concepts.explanation: Length is the distance from one end of an object to the other, measured along its longest side. When we ask how long something is, we are asking about its length. A pencil, a piece of rope, a shelf and the road to the market all have a length. But length is not the only measurement an object has. The same object can be measured in different directions, and each direction has its own special word:
concepts.example: Length – the distance along the longest side (how long).

concepts.title: Standard Units – Centimetres and Metres
concepts.explanation: Long ago, people measured things using their hands, their feet or their arms. This was fun, but it caused problems. If Hassan measures a table with his hands and Omar measures the same table with his hands, they will get different answers – because Omar's hands are bigger! When Hassan says the table is 20 hands wide and Omar says it is 18 hands wide, who is right? They both are, but nobody can agree on the true size. This is exactly why we need standard units that are the same for everyone. In this unit we use two…
concepts.example: A centimetre is a small unit, about the width of your little finger.

concepts.title: Using a Ruler Correctly
concepts.explanation: A ruler is a wonderful tool, but only if you use it correctly. Many measuring mistakes come from using the ruler in the wrong way, so let us learn the right way carefully. The first thing to check is where your ruler begins. Rulers are not all the same. Look closely at the start of yours:
concepts.example: Some rulers begin measuring right at the edge – the very edge is the 0 point.

concepts.title: Estimating Before You Measure
concepts.explanation: A clever habit that all good measurers keep is to estimate first. An estimate is a sensible guess about a length, made before you measure carefully. It is not a wild number – it is a thoughtful guess based on what you can see and what you already know about sizes. Why bother estimating if you are going to measure anyway? Because an estimate warns you if your measurement goes badly wrong. If Idris estimates that his table is about 80 cm wide, and then his ruler seems to say 300 cm, he knows at once that something…
concepts.example: Here is the simple method for estimating and measuring:

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Lengths
concepts.explanation: Once you can measure, you can compare lengths to say which is bigger or smaller. We have special comparing words for each kind of measurement, and using the right word shows that you truly understand what you are measuring. Longer / shorter – used to compare lengths. “The blue pencil is longer than the red pencil.”
concepts.example: Taller / shorter – used to compare heights. “Yusuf is taller than his little sister.”

concepts.title: Drawing Lines of a Given Length
concepts.explanation: Measuring is not only about objects that already exist – you can also use a ruler to draw a line of an exact length. This is a skill you will use in mathematics, in art and in many other subjects, so it is worth doing carefully. To draw a line of a chosen length, for example 5 centimetres, follow these steps:
concepts.example: Place your ruler flat on the paper and hold it firmly so it will not slip.

methods.title: How to measure an object
methods.example: Would you measure the length of a door in centimetres or metres?
steps: Find the 0 mark on your ruler.
Line the 0 mark up with one end of the object.
Keep the ruler straight and hold it steady.
Read the number at the other end – that is the length in centimetres.

methods.title: How to draw a line of an exact length
methods.example: Aisha lays her pencil on a ruler. The 0 mark is lined up with one end, and the other end reaches the 13 mark. How long is the pencil?
steps: Hold the ruler firmly on the paper.
Make a small mark at the 0 point.
Make a second mark at the length you want (for 5 cm, mark at 5).
Draw a straight line joining the two marks, then measure to check.

methods.title: How to estimate then measure
methods.example: A line on Farah's page runs from the 2 mark to the 10 mark on her ruler. How long is the line?
steps: Look at the object and make a sensible guess.
Write your estimate down.
Measure carefully with a ruler or metre stick.
Compare your estimate to the real length.

methods.title: Compare two lengths
methods.example: Amina's ribbon is 45 cm and Maryam's ribbon is 38 cm. Whose ribbon is longer, and by how much?
steps: Solution: Compare the numbers: 45 is more than 38, so Amina's ribbon is longer.
To find how much longer, take the difference: 45 − 38 = 7 cm.
Amina's ribbon is 7 cm longer.

methods.title: The rope problem (difference)
methods.example: A rope is 24 metres long. Yusuf cuts off 14 metres. How much rope is left?
steps: Solution: The rope that is left is the difference between what he started with and what he cut off.
24 − 14 = 10 metres.
There are 10 metres of rope left.

methods.title: Order lengths from shortest to longest
methods.example: Hassan measures four sticks: 12 cm, 30 cm, 8 cm and 21 cm. Put them in order from shortest to longest.
steps: Solution: Find the smallest first, then the next, and so on.
The order from shortest to longest is 8 cm, 12 cm, 21 cm, 30 cm.
Check the order lengths from shortest to longest result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Choose the right unit
workedExamples.prompt: Would you measure the length of a door in centimetres or metres?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A door is a large object, so we use the larger unit. The best choice is metres. A door is about 2 metres tall. Measuring it in centimetres would give a big awkward number like 200 cm, so metres is more sensible.

workedExamples.title: Read a ruler correctly
workedExamples.prompt: Aisha lays her pencil on a ruler. The 0 mark is lined up with one end, and the other end reaches the 13 mark. How long is the pencil?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Because the pencil starts at 0 and ends at 13, its length is simply 13 centimetres. Each mark on the ruler stands for one centimetre, so we read the number at the far end.

workedExamples.title: A line that does not start at zero
workedExamples.prompt: A line on Farah's page runs from the 2 mark to the 10 mark on her ruler. How long is the line?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The line does not start at 0, so we find the difference. Take the start away from the end: 10 − 2 = 8 centimetres. The line is 8 cm long, not 10 cm.

workedExamples.title: Compare two lengths
workedExamples.prompt: Amina's ribbon is 45 cm and Maryam's ribbon is 38 cm. Whose ribbon is longer, and by how much?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Compare the numbers: 45 is more than 38, so Amina's ribbon is longer. To find how much longer, take the difference: 45 − 38 = 7 cm. Amina's ribbon is 7 cm longer.

workedExamples.title: The rope problem (difference)
workedExamples.prompt: A rope is 24 metres long. Yusuf cuts off 14 metres. How much rope is left?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The rope that is left is the difference between what he started with and what he cut off. 24 − 14 = 10 metres. There are 10 metres of rope left.

workedExamples.title: Order lengths from shortest to longest
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan measures four sticks: 12 cm, 30 cm, 8 cm and 21 cm. Put them in order from shortest to longest.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the smallest first, then the next, and so on. The order from shortest to longest is 8 cm, 12 cm, 21 cm, 30 cm.

workedExamples.title: The ladder problem (joining lengths)
workedExamples.prompt: Two ladders are 5 metres and 12 metres long. If they are joined together, how tall are they altogether? Could you make exactly 17 metres?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Joining lengths means adding them. 5 + 12 = 17 metres. Yes! Joining the 5 m ladder and the 12 m ladder makes exactly 17 metres.

workedExamples.title: Metres and centimetres together
workedExamples.prompt: Which is longer, 90 centimetres or 1 metre?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Change to the same unit first. 1 metre = 100 centimetres. Now compare 90 cm and 100 cm. Since 100 is more than 90, 1 metre is longer than 90 centimetres, by 10 cm.

workedExamples.title: Fill in: 1 m = ___ cm, and 2 m = ___ cm
workedExamples.prompt: Fill in: 1 m = ___ cm, and 2 m = ___ cm.

workedExamples.prompt: Halima's ribbon is 30 cm. She cuts off 12 cm. How much is left?

activities.title: Home Measuring Hunt
activities.materials: a ruler and a pencil and paper.
steps: Choose five small objects at home – a spoon, a book, a comb, a leaf and your hand.
For each one, first estimate its length in centimetres and write your guess down.
Now measure each object carefully with your ruler, starting from the 0 mark.
Compare your estimate to the real length. How close were you? Did your guesses get better?
Keep your list – you can see how your estimating improves over the week.

activities.title: Make Your Own Ruler
activities.materials: 15 or more centimetre cubes (or small equal blocks), a strip of paper, and a pencil.
steps: Lay the cubes in a straight line along the strip of paper, with the first cube at the left edge.
The left edge is your 0. Where two cubes meet, draw a line on the paper.
Keep adding cubes and drawing lines until you reach the end of the paper.
Number each mark 1, 2, 3, and write ‘centimetres’ on your ruler. Now measure things with it!
This shows you how a real ruler is made, and why each centimetre is exactly the same length.

activities.title: The Longer or Shorter Game
activities.materials: paper cards with lengths written on them (like 12 cm, 30 cm, 8 cm, 45 cm).
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down.
Each player turns over one card.
Say who has the longer length and who has the shorter length.
The player with the longer length keeps both cards. Play until the cards run out.
This game trains you to compare lengths quickly and use the words longer and shorter.

activities.title: How Long Is a Piece of String? (Investigation)
activities.materials: a piece of string 1 metre long, a ruler or metre stick, scissors and paper. Work with a partner and take turns.
steps: Estimate how much string you want to cut off, then cut it.
Measure the piece you cut and write down both your estimate and the real length.
Repeat three times each, cutting from the string that is left.
At the end, measure the string that remains. Talk about why measuring from the start of the ruler matters.
This investigation shows how important it is to measure carefully from the very start of the scale each time.

activities.title: Height and Arm Span (Family Investigation)
activities.materials: a metre stick or tape measure, and members of your family.
steps: Stretch your arms out wide and ask someone to measure the distance from fingertip to fingertip – this is your arm span.
Now measure your height from head to toe.
Compare them: if your arm span is longer than your height, you are a ‘wide rectangle’; if they are equal, you are a ‘square’; if your height is longer, you are a ‘tall rectangle’.
Measure your whole family. Is everyone the same shape? Most people are close to a square!

activities.title: Paper Strip Rings (Design Project)
activities.materials: a sheet of paper, a ruler, a pencil, scissors and glue or tape.
steps: Draw a strip 20 cm long and 4 cm wide. Measure carefully!
Draw a second strip 5 cm shorter and 1 cm narrower (15 cm by 3 cm), then a third (10 cm by 2 cm), then a fourth (5 cm by 1 cm).
Cut out the four strips and bend each one into a ring, sticking the ends together.
See if the rings fit inside one another. Then decorate them!

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand measuring best? What was the trickiest part of using a ruler? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Units of Length Rule
rules.text: 1 metre = 100 centimetres (1 m = 100 cm). Centimetres for short things, metres for long things.

rules.title: Using a Ruler Rule
rules.text: Find the 0 mark first. Line it up with one end of the object. Read the number at the other end. Each mark stands for one centimetre.

rules.title: Line Not Starting at Zero Rule
rules.text: Length equals the end number take away the start number. A line from 3 to 9 is 9 take away 3, which is 6 centimetres.

terms: How tall or high something is
A small unit, about a little-finger width
A large unit, about one big step; 100 cm
How much longer one length is than another
commonMistakes: Starting at the edge, not the 0
Always find the 0 mark and start there
Reading a line as its end number
Take the start number away from the end
Change to the same unit: 1 m = 100 cm
Use metres for long things, cm for short

realProblems.prompt: At Eastleigh market, Khadija buys cloth 100 cm long and uses 45 cm for a headscarf. How many centimetres are left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Omar measures his height as just over 1 metre. His little brother is 85 cm. Who is taller, and by about how much?
realProblems.answer: Omar is taller; he is just over 100 cm and his brother is 85 cm, so about 15 cm taller.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Omar is taller; he is just over 100 cm and his brother is 85 cm, so about 15 cm taller.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A line runs from the 6 mark to the 20 mark on a ruler. How long is it?
Ladders measure 5 m, 8 m, 12 m and 3 m. Which ladders join together to make exactly 20 metres?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The 5 m, 12 m and 3 m ladders (5 + 12 + 3 = 20 m).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A rope is 24 metres long. If 14 metres is cut off, how much is left? (Find the difference.)
Zahra draws a strip 20 cm long, then a second strip 5 cm shorter, then a third strip 5 cm shorter again. How long is the third strip?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 20 − 5 = 15 cm, then 15 − 5 = 10 cm; the third strip is 10 cm.

We have special comparing words for each kind of measurement, and using the right word shows that you truly understand what you are measuring. Longer / shorter – used to compare lengths. “The blue pencil is longer than the red pencil.”

questions.question: A line on Farah's page runs from the 2 mark to the 10 mark on her ruler. How long is the line?

questions.explanation: The line does not start at 0, so we find the difference. Take the start away from the end: 10 − 2 = 8 centimetres. The line is 8 cm long, not 10 cm.

questions.question: What does “Length” mean?
options: How tall or high something is
A small unit, about a little-finger width
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Length means How long something is (longest side).

questions.question: What does “Width” mean?
options: How tall or high something is
A small unit, about a little-finger width
A large unit, about one big step; 100 cm
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Width means How wide something is (shorter side).

questions.question: What does “Height” mean?
options: How tall or high something is
A small unit, about a little-finger width
A large unit, about one big step; 100 cm
questions.answer: How tall or high something is
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Height means How tall or high something is.

rounds.prompt: A line on Farah's page runs from the 2 mark to the 10 mark on her ruler. How long is the line?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
rounds.explanation: The line does not start at 0, so we find the difference. Take the start away from the end: 10 − 2 = 8 centimetres. The line is 8 cm long, not 10 cm.

rounds.prompt: A line runs from the 6 mark to the 20 mark on a ruler. How long is it?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.

rounds.prompt: How many centimetres are in 3 metres?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

rounds.prompt: What does “Length” mean?
choices: How long something is (longest side)
How wide something is (shorter side)
How tall or high something is
A small unit, about a little-finger width
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Length means How long something is (longest side).

rounds.prompt: A rope is 24 metres long. Yusuf cuts off 14 metres. How much rope is left?

rounds.explanation: The rope that is left is the difference between what he started with and what he cut off. 24 − 14 = 10 metres. There are 10 metres of rope left.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and whenever you can, pick up a real ruler and measure the very things we talk about. Mathematics about measuring is best learned with a ruler in your hand, not just by reading. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers, and your teacher will meet with you about twice a week to help with anything tricky.

I can why does measuring matter so much? Length is everywhere in daily life. Traders measure cloth and rope to sell the right amount. Carpenters measure wood before they cut, because a piece cut too short is wasted. Farmers measure how far apart to plant seeds. Families measure children to see how much they have grown. Even builders raising the walls of a new home at Nuur Academy must measure carefully, or the building will not be straight. When you become confident with measuring length, you are gaining a skill that people all around you use every single day.

I can as you work, keep a ruler, a pencil and some paper beside you. Draw the lines, measure real objects, and say the measurements out loud. If you measure something wrongly, that is not a failure – it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Measuring well is a skill of the hands and the eyes as much as the mind, and like any skill, it grows stronger every time you use it.
I can use the standard units centimetres (cm) and metres (m) to measure length.
I can explain that there are 100 centimetres in 1 metre.
I can use a ruler and a metre stick correctly, starting from the right place.
I can estimate a length first, then measure to check how close you were.
I can tell the difference between length, width and height.
